summary: Justin Brennan conducts a mentorship Q&A focused on financing before raising capital. He reviews due diligence checklists and underwriting documents with mentees, analyzes two specific multifamily deals (16-unit Lansing MI and 28-unit Louisville KY), and provides detailed guidance on deal structuring, loan assumptions, expense modeling, and lender pre-approval letters needed for LOIs.
takeaways: Separate property taxes and insurance from loan payments in underwriting—these are operational expenses that must be listed separately, not wrapped into PITI
Use the 75% break-even metric: if 25%+ of units go vacant, you should still cover all expenses and mortgage payments
For underwriting benchmarks: 1% rule, 75% rule, IRR analysis, and check if property relies on appreciation or cash flow
Section 8 subsidy is better when 70% subsidized (tenant pays 30%) vs 100% paid—subsidy travels with tenant, giving them incentive to maintain unit and keep rental assistance
Due diligence period should be 28-30 days minimum, not 2 weeks—insufficient time to review all leases, financials, inspections, and secure investor commitments
Request lender quote on official letterhead stating loan amount contingent on standard conditions—frame it as 'lender quote' not 'pre-approval' to reduce lender friction
Closing cost estimate should include $10K for legal (not lower), plus standard appraisal, environmental, title, insurance, escrow for 6 months
Operating reserves: plug 50K capex reserve minimum for older properties to reduce equity requirement; renovations typically 11K per unit for 28-unit example

skill_candidates.change: Separate debt components in expense models to isolate property taxes and insurance from loan principal/interest
skill_candidates.evidence: Kevin's response about segmenting property taxes, insurance, and loan payments separately in the expense model so operational expenses are clearly distinguished from financing costs

skill_candidates.change: Validate rent growth and expense assumptions by requesting detailed unit-level rent rolls and verifying tax/insurance costs don't track property age
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin's review of Alan's Lansing deal emphasized verifying the $250-$400 rent bump was achievable naturally, catching that insurance was low for building age, and recommending increasing it to $14K

skill_candidates.change: Apply the 75% break-even rule and 10% vacancy/credit loss floor as underwriting guardrails
skill_candidates.evidence: Michael's Louisville deal discussion: Justin validated the 75% occupancy break-even target and recommended 10% vacancy assumption across all years as conservative baseline for market metrics

skill_candidates.change: Request 28-30 day due diligence periods instead of accepting 2-week timelines by conditioning on full financial access
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin's response: tell brokers 'I can do it in 2 weeks if you give me Google Drive access to all leases, financials, and bank accounts'—forcing them to either extend timeline or admit other competing buyers exist

title: Tuesday Mentorship Q&A - Financing Before Raising Capital_02-03-26 | Justin Brennan
